cards.name: Pomegranate
cards.description: A fruit that hides hundreds of seeds behind tough leather — abundance wrapped in effort.
cards.text: What in you today is ready to be opened, but only by someone willing to stain their hands?

cards.name: Peach
cards.description: Fuzzed skin, soft flesh, hard stone — a body that insists on softness and still keeps a pit.
cards.text: Consider the places you have been tender today. Notice where the stone still is beneath them.

cards.name: Grape
cards.description: A fruit that lives most of its life in clusters and only becomes itself alone.
cards.text: Not all those who wander are lost.

cards.name: Mango
cards.description: A fruit you cannot eat politely — the sweetness demands your whole face.
cards.text: Where in your work have you been trying to stay tidy, and what would it cost you to give in?

cards.name: Watermelon
cards.description: Mostly water, loud with sugar, green on the outside and impossibly pink within.
cards.text: Sit with how much of you is water. Let that be the reason you bend instead of break.

cards.name: Coconut
cards.description: A fruit with three faces — armour, milk, and meat — each one asking a different kind of labour.
cards.text: What part of you today needs armour, what part needs feeding, and who gets to ask which?

cards.name: Persimmon
cards.description: A fruit that is astringent until it is ripe, then astonishing — patience as a flavour.
cards.text: Between stimulus and response there is a space. In that space is our power to choose our response.

cards.name: Pear
cards.description: A fruit shaped like a drop that hasn't fallen — poised, weighted, almost going.
cards.text: What in you is nearly ready to fall, and what would it mean to stop catching it?

cards.name: Blackberry
cards.description: A fruit that grows from thorns and stains whoever it touches — sweetness through defence.
cards.text: Think of a person whose care has pricked you. Hold that the thorn and the fruit may not be separable.

cards.name: Olive
cards.description: A fruit bitter raw — it becomes itself only through time, salt, and pressure.
cards.text: What in your practice is still bitter because it hasn't yet been cured, and who is rushing it?

cards.name: Apricot
cards.description: A small golden fruit with a bitter almond hidden at the centre — sweetness with a secret.
cards.text: Tell me, what is it you plan to do with your one wild and precious life?

cards.name: Passion Fruit
cards.description: A wrinkled shell around a loud, wet interior — a fruit that looks spent and still isn't.
cards.text: Where are you judging your aliveness by what your surface looks like today?

cards.name: Quince
cards.description: Inedible until cooked — a fruit that insists on being accompanied by fire.
cards.text: Notice what in your life refuses to become nourishing until someone has stayed with it through heat.

cards.name: Strawberry
cards.description: A fruit whose seeds ride on the outside — the vulnerable bits on display.
cards.text: What are you carrying today where the seeds are showing, and who is it safe to let see them?

cards.name: Orange
cards.description: Sectioned into neat chambers under its skin — a fruit that is already divided, waiting.
cards.text: Consider how much of you is already portioned into the roles others hold of you. Which chamber gets the first bite?

cards.name: Fig
cards.description: A fruit that blooms inside itself — flower and fruit as the same private room.
cards.text: There is another world, but it is in this one.

cards.name: Lemon
cards.description: A fruit you almost never eat alone — it is meant to change whatever it joins.
cards.text: Where is your role in the room today to become part of something else rather than stand on your own?

cards.name: Cherry
cards.description: Small, brief, and doubled on its stem — a fruit that arrives with a companion and leaves fast.
cards.text: Name one brief thing you loved this week. Let the brevity be part of why it counted.

cards.name: Plum
cards.description: A fruit whose skin holds a bruise the flesh doesn't share — visible history, private interior.
cards.text: I have eaten the plums that were in the icebox. Forgive me. They were delicious, so sweet and so cold.
cards.attribution: William Carlos Williams, This Is Just To Say

cards.name: Apple
cards.description: The most familiar fruit in the room, the one whose meaning everyone already thinks they know.
cards.text: What in your work has become so familiar that you have stopped actually tasting it?
